q: What happens when you call result.get here?

opts.t: Throws NoSuchElementException at runtime
Prints None safely
opts.why: Option.get on None throws — it does not print a default value.

opts.t: Prints null
opts.why: Scala Option never holds null; None.get throws NoSuchElementException.

why: Option.get on a None throws java.util.NoSuchElementException. Always use getOrElse, fold, or pattern-match instead of naked .get to handle the missing case safely.
fj: "Option.get is safe — it just returns None if empty."
fs: "Never call Option.get without a guard; use getOrElse() or pattern-match on Some/None."

q: What does this print?

opts.why: y captures the value 10 at assignment; reassigning x does not affect y.

opts.t: Compile error — val cannot hold an Int
opts.why: val can hold any type including Int; it simply cannot be reassigned.

why: val binds to the value at the time of assignment. y = x copies the integer value 10 into y. Later reassigning the var x to 20 has no effect on y.
fj: "val is like a pointer — reassigning x updates y too."
fs: "val captures the value at binding time; it never tracks a var after the fact."

q: Why does eq return false but == return true here?

opts.t: (A) false, (B) true
(A) true, (B) true
opts.why: eq tests reference identity; two new String() calls produce different objects, so eq is false.

opts.t: (A) false, (B) false
opts.why: == in Scala delegates to equals(), which compares String content; s1 and s2 have equal content so (B) is true.

why: In Scala, == delegates to equals() (null-safe), so it compares string content and returns true. eq tests reference identity (like Java's ==), so two separately allocated String objects give false.
fj: "eq and == do the same thing in Scala."
fs: "== is value equality (calls equals); eq is reference identity — use == for content comparison."

q: What is wrong with this string interpolation?

opts.t: Prints literal "Hello $name" — interpolation needs s prefix
Compile error — $ is not valid in a String
opts.why: $ inside a plain String literal is just a character; it compiles fine but does not interpolate.

opts.t: Prints "Hello World"
opts.why: Without the s prefix the $ is treated as a literal dollar sign, not an interpolation marker.

why: Scala string interpolation requires the s prefix: s"Hello $name". A plain double-quoted string treats $ as a literal character. Other valid prefixes are f (formatted) and raw (no escape processing).
fj: "$ in any Scala string triggers interpolation automatically."
fs: "Add the s prefix — s\"Hello $name\" — to enable string interpolation."

q: What is the evaluation order problem here?

opts.t: Compile error — base is defined twice
url uses the second base definition
opts.why: Scala does not allow duplicate val definitions in the same scope; this fails to compile.

opts.t: url is always http://api/v2 because lazy val is fixed at first access
opts.why: The code never compiles due to the duplicate definition.

why: Scala disallows duplicate val (or lazy val) names in the same scope. The compiler rejects this with a 'base is already defined' error. Lazy val defers initialization but does not allow redefinition.
fj: "The second lazy val shadows the first like a var reassignment."
fs: "lazy val is still a val — duplicate definitions in the same scope are a compile error."

q: What does this evaluate to?

opts.t: Some(Some(6)), Some(6)
Some(6), Some(6)
opts.why: map does not flatten; it wraps the result in another Some, giving Some(Some(6)).

opts.t: Some(Some(6)), Some(Some(6))
opts.why: flatMap flattens one layer, so it returns Some(6) not Some(Some(6)).

why: map applies the function and wraps the result: map(x => Some(x*2)) gives Some(Some(6)). flatMap applies then flattens one level, so Some(3).flatMap(x => Some(x*2)) gives Some(6). Use flatMap when your function already returns an Option.
fj: "map and flatMap on Option always produce the same type."
fs: "Use flatMap when your transform returns an Option; map + flatten equals flatMap."

q: Why does the compiler warn about this match?
lines: def label(c: Color): String = c match {

opts.t: match may not be exhaustive — Blue is unhandled
sealed trait cannot be used in a match expression
opts.why: sealed traits are specifically designed for exhaustive matching; using them in match is idiomatic.

opts.t: No warning — the match compiles silently and returns null for Blue
opts.why: Scala warns on non-exhaustive sealed matches; at runtime Blue would throw MatchError, not return null.

why: Because Color is sealed, the compiler knows all subtypes and warns when a match is non-exhaustive. At runtime, passing Blue throws MatchError. Add a case Blue => or a wildcard case _ => to fix.
fj: "Non-exhaustive sealed match just returns null for the missing case."
fs: "Sealed type + match = compiler exhaustiveness check; missing cases are a warning (error with -Xfatal-warnings)."

q: Which overload does the compiler call?

opts.t: Compile error — ambiguous reference to overloaded definition
Hello Mr Lee — default-arg overload wins
opts.why: The compiler cannot decide which overload to use; it does not prefer default-arg versions.

opts.t: Hey Lee — single-arg overload wins
opts.why: Both overloads match the call; Scala reports ambiguity rather than silently picking one.

why: Scala's overload resolution sees two candidates for greet("Lee"): the single-arg overload and the two-arg overload with a default. This is ambiguous and the compiler rejects it. Avoid combining default arguments with overloads that have the same arity after defaults.
fj: "Default args make the single-arg overload the obvious winner."
fs: "Default-arg + same-arity overload = compile-time ambiguity; pick one or rename."

q: When does the Future's computation start?

opts.t: Immediately when Future { } is constructed
When Await.result or onComplete is called
opts.why: Future eagerly submits the body to the ExecutionContext at construction, not when consumed.

opts.t: On the next scheduler tick after the current call stack unwinds
opts.why: The task is submitted to the thread pool immediately; scheduling depends on pool availability, not a defer mechanism.

why: scala.concurrent.Future is eager: the body is submitted to the ExecutionContext the moment the Future {} block is evaluated. This differs from lazy abstractions like IO in cats-effect or ZIO, which defer execution until explicitly run.
fj: "Future is lazy — nothing runs until you call Await or register a callback."
fs: "Future starts immediately; use cats IO or ZIO for deferred/lazy execution semantics."

q: Which implicit Ordering is picked, and is that surprising?

opts.t: Sorted by length: List(c, bb, aaa)
Sorted lexicographically: List(aaa, bb, c)
opts.why: The local implicit strLen shadows the default Ordering.String, so length-based sort wins.

opts.t: Compile error — ambiguous implicits
opts.why: There is no ambiguity; the local implicit in the current scope wins over the companion-object implicit.

why: Scala implicit resolution prefers a locally defined implicit over one in the companion object or predef. strLen shadows Ordering.String, so sorted uses length ordering. Shadowing default implicits is a common subtle bug when the local definition is in a utility object imported wholesale.
fj: "sorted always uses lexicographic order for strings."
fs: "A local implicit shadows the default; import carefully to avoid silent ordering changes."

q: How many elements are evaluated before the take?

opts.t: Exactly 2 — Iterator is lazy, map runs on demand
All elements — map eagerly processes the infinite iterator
opts.why: Iterator.map is lazy; it only evaluates elements as they are pulled by take(2).toList.

opts.t: Compile error — infinite Iterator cannot be mapped
opts.why: Scala allows mapping infinite iterators lazily; it only fails if you call toList without take.

why: Iterator.map returns a new lazy iterator; it does not evaluate elements upfront. take(2).toList pulls exactly two elements, so map runs twice and prints 'map 1' and 'map 2'. This contrasts with List.map which is strict and processes all elements immediately.
fj: "map on a collection always processes all elements eagerly."
fs: "Iterator.map is lazy (pull-based); List.map is strict (push-all). Know which you're using."

q: What is the data race risk here?

opts.t: count is a shared var mutated concurrently — result is unpredictable
Prints 1000 — Future.sequence waits for all to finish
opts.why: Awaiting completion does not make the increments atomic; concurrent reads/writes to an unprotected var produce lost updates.

opts.t: Throws ConcurrentModificationException
opts.why: That exception is Java collection specific; a Scala var race corrupts the value silently, not via exception.

why: var count is captured by reference in the closures. Multiple threads execute count += 1 (read-increment-write) concurrently without synchronization, causing lost updates. Fix: use an AtomicInteger or a single-threaded aggregation with foldLeft after awaiting.
fj: "Future.sequence waits for all futures so the counter is consistent."
fs: "var + concurrent Futures = data race; use AtomicInteger or aggregate after completion."

q: Why does this Spark job fail with serialization errors?

opts.t: Spark serializes the whole Transformer including non-serializable helper
Only the lambda x => x * factor is serialized — helper is safe
opts.why: The lambda captures the enclosing Transformer instance (this) to reach factor, pulling in helper too.

opts.t: Serializable on the class prevents any serialization error
opts.why: Serializable is inherited, but a non-serializable field still causes NotSerializableException at runtime.

why: Spark serializes the closure x => x * factor. To access factor the closure captures this (the Transformer instance). Because helper is a field of Transformer and DBHelper is not Serializable, the serialization of Transformer fails. Fix: copy factor to a local val before the map call: val f = factor; rdd.map(x => x * f).
fj: "Marking the class Serializable is enough — field types don't matter."
fs: "Extract needed fields to local vals before closures to avoid pulling in non-serializable class members."

q: What prod problem does this code introduce?

opts.t: Blocking inside global EC starves the thread pool
Await.result is forbidden in production Scala code
opts.why: It's not forbidden, but blocking on the global EC in a high-throughput service causes thread starvation.

opts.t: 30-second timeout is too long and causes memory leaks
opts.why: The timeout duration alone doesn't cause memory leaks; the starvation happens regardless of the duration.

why: global EC is a fixed-size ForkJoinPool. Calling Await.result blocks a pool thread, preventing it from handling other Futures. Under load, all threads block on network I/O and new Futures queue indefinitely (thread starvation). Fix: keep the async boundary — use flatMap/for-comprehension and never block inside a Future running on the global EC.
fj: "Await.result with a timeout is safe because threads are released after the timeout."
fs: "Never block inside the global EC; keep async or use a dedicated blocking dispatcher."

q: This case class breaks HashMap contracts. Why?

opts.t: (A) false, (B) false — Array uses reference equality
(A) true, (B) true — case class generates deep equals
opts.why: Scala case class generates equals using each field's own equals; Array.equals is reference-based, not element-wise.

opts.t: (A) true, (B) false — equals is deep but hashCode is not
opts.why: Both equals and hashCode delegate to Array, which is reference-based, so both are false for distinct Array instances.

why: Scala's generated case class equals calls each field's equals method. Array.equals is Java's Object.equals — reference identity. So r1 != r2 even with identical contents. hashCode also uses Array's identity-based hashCode. Use Seq or Vector instead of Array in case classes, or manually override equals/hashCode.
fj: "Case class always deep-compares arrays element by element."
fs: "Never put Arrays in case classes; use Seq/Vector or override equals+hashCode manually."

q: Where is the hidden performance trap?

opts.t: Allocates 1 million RichInt objects via implicit conversion
filter is lazy so no objects are allocated until toList
opts.why: Range.filter is strict and evaluates eagerly on a Range; the implicit conversion fires for each element.

opts.t: Implicit conversions are inlined by the compiler — no allocation
opts.why: Only value classes (extends AnyVal) can be unboxed/inlined; a regular class like RichInt allocates on the heap.

why: The implicit conversion allocates a new RichInt wrapper for each of the million integers before calling isEven. To avoid allocation, extend AnyVal: class RichInt(val n: Int) extends AnyVal — the compiler then inlines the method call and eliminates the object. This is the extension method pattern in Scala 2 (Scala 3 uses extension directly).
fj: "Implicit conversions are zero-cost — the compiler optimizes them away."
fs: "Extend AnyVal for zero-allocation implicit wrappers; plain classes allocate per use."

q: What blast-radius risk does this library API have?

opts.t: The API leaks implicit Ordering as a hidden contract callers must satisfy
The method is fine — implicit is resolved transparently
opts.why: It 'works' for primitives, but callers with custom types must provide an Ordering. The API doesn't document this, creating invisible breakage for non-primitive types.

opts.t: Compile error — implicit in a library method is not allowed
opts.why: Implicits are valid in library methods; the problem is the undocumented constraint on callers.

why: Requiring an implicit Ordering[T] puts a non-obvious burden on all callers: they must either have an Ordering in scope or the call fails at compile time with a cryptic error. In a shared library, document the implicit requirement explicitly (Scaladoc, type constraint), or constrain T with a context bound like T: Ordering to make it obvious at the call site.
fj: "Implicit parameters are invisible to callers — great for ergonomics in libraries."
fs: "Implicit constraints are part of your public API contract; document them or use T: Ordering syntax to surface them."

q: What binary compatibility hazard does this trait addition cause?
lines: // shared-lib v2 — adding a new method

opts.t: Adding an abstract method breaks binary compat — all implementors must recompile
Trait members are duck-typed so no recompile needed
opts.why: Scala traits compile to JVM interfaces; adding an abstract method without a default breaks existing compiled implementors.

opts.t: Adding validate is fine because it has a different name
opts.why: The name is irrelevant; any new abstract method forces all downstream implementors to implement it or fail at link-time.

why: In Scala 2, traits compile to interfaces. Adding an abstract method to a published trait breaks all existing compiled classes that implement it — they fail with AbstractMethodError at runtime without recompilation. Fix: provide a default implementation (def validate(...) = true) to preserve binary compatibility, or use sealed traits within a module boundary.
fj: "Adding new methods to a trait is always backward compatible."
fs: "New abstract trait methods break binary compat; provide defaults or version the trait."

q: Why is exposing the global ExecutionContext in a library dangerous?

opts.t: Library hard-codes the EC; callers cannot inject their own and thread pools collide
global is the best EC — libraries should always use it for consistency
opts.why: Hard-coding global prevents callers from using a dedicated thread pool for I/O vs CPU work, and causes contention.

opts.t: Future without an explicit EC is a compile error in Scala
opts.why: Future requires an implicit EC; importing global satisfies that — the problem is ownership, not compilation.

why: A library that hard-codes global prevents callers from controlling thread-pool sizing and dispatch strategy. The caller's EC context (e.g., Akka dispatcher, Cats ContextShift) is ignored. The idiomatic fix is to accept an implicit ExecutionContext parameter and let callers provide it.
fj: "Libraries should use global so callers don't need to pass an ExecutionContext."
fs: "Accept implicit ExecutionContext as a parameter; never hard-code global in library APIs."

q: What is the blast-radius flaw in this config approach?

opts.t: Stringly-typed config breaks silently at runtime, not at startup
sys.env is thread-unsafe and will corrupt reads under load
opts.why: sys.env reads the JVM environment which is effectively immutable at runtime; no concurrency issue there.

opts.t: The exception message leaks config key names — a security issue only
opts.why: Leaking key names is a minor concern; the main structural problem is stringly-typed, runtime-discovered config across many services.

why: Stringly-typed config (get("DB_HOST")) defers validation to runtime and scatters key names as magic strings across 40 services. A typo or renamed variable silently deploys and only blows up when the code path executes in production. Fix: define a typed Config ADT validated at startup (e.g., with Ciris or PureConfig) so misconfiguration is caught at boot, not during a 3am incident.
fj: "String-keyed config is flexible and works fine at scale."
fs: "Stringly-typed config fails at runtime not compile time; validate with a typed ADT at startup across all consumers."
